diagrams.description: Diagram showing the radial nature of the electric field around an infinitely long charged wire. Points $P, P', P''$ at the same radial distance from the wire have the same field magnitude due to symmetry.

subsections.title: Flux Calculation for a Cylindrical Gaussian Surface
subsections.content_text: To calculate the field, imagine a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $l$ coaxial with the wire. Since the field is everywhere radial, it is parallel to the surface normals of the curved part of the cylinder but perpendicular to the normals of the two flat end caps. Consequently, the flux through the two ends of the cylindrical Gaussian surface is zero. At the curved part of the surface, $\mathbf{E}$ is normal to the surface at every point, and its magnitude is constant because it depends only on $r$. The surface area of the curved part is $2\pi rl$, where $l$ is the length of the cylinder. Flux through the Gaussian surface = flux through the curved cylindrical part of the surface = $E \times 2\pi rl$.

diagrams.label: Figure 1.26(b)
diagrams.description: A cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $l$ surrounding a long thin wire of uniform linear charge density. The electric field $\mathbf{E}$ is shown perpendicular to the curved surface and parallel to the end caps.

subsections.title: Applying Gauss's Law
subsections.content_text: The surface includes charge equal to $\lambda l$. Gauss’s law then gives $E \times 2\pi rl = \lambda l / \epsilon_0$, which simplifies to $E = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\epsilon_0 r}$.

subsections.title: Vector Form and Direction
subsections.content_text: Vectorially, the electric field $\mathbf{E}$ at any point is given by $\mathbf{E} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\epsilon_0 r} \hat{\mathbf{n}}$ (1.32), where $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is the radial unit vector in the plane normal to the wire passing through the point. $\mathbf{E}$ is directed outward if $\lambda$ is positive and inward if $\lambda$ is negative.

subsections.title: Algebraic Representation of Vectors
subsections.content_text: When a vector $\mathbf{A}$ is written as a scalar multiplied by a unit vector ($\mathbf{A} = A \hat{\mathbf{a}}$), the scalar $A$ is an algebraic number. The direction of $\mathbf{A}$ is the same as $\hat{\mathbf{a}}$ if $A > 0$ and opposite if $A < 0$. The modulus $|\mathbf{A}|$ is used for non-negative values, so $|\mathbf{A}| \ge 0$.

subsections.title: Physical Considerations and Assumptions
subsections.content_text: Although only the enclosed charge $\lambda l$ is used in the calculation, the electric field $\mathbf{E}$ is due to the charge on the entire wire. The assumption of an infinitely long wire is crucial for $\mathbf{E}$ to be normal to the curved part of the Gaussian surface. However, the result is approximately true for the central portions of a long wire where end effects can be ignored.

sections.title: Field due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet

subsections.title: Gaussian Surface and Flux Calculation
subsections.content_text: The Gaussian surface is taken as a rectangular parallelepiped of cross-sectional area $A$. Only the two faces (1 and 2) normal to the $x$-axis contribute to the flux, as field lines are parallel to the other faces. The unit vector normal to surface 1 is in the $-x$ direction, and for surface 2, it is in the $+x$ direction. Thus, the flux through both surfaces is equal and adds up to a net flux of $2EA$.

subsections.title: Independence of Distance
subsections.content_text: The application of Gauss's law shows that the electric field $E$ is independent of the distance $x$ from the sheet. For a finite large planar sheet, this result is approximately true in the middle regions, away from the ends.

sections.title: Field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell

subsections.title: Spherical Symmetry and Setup
subsections.content_text: Let $\sigma$ be the uniform surface charge density of a thin spherical shell of radius $R$. Due to spherical symmetry, the field at any point $P$ depends only on the radial distance $r$ from the center and must be directed along the radius vector.

diagrams.label: Figure 1.28
diagrams.description: Gaussian surfaces for a thin spherical shell: (a) for a point outside (r > R) and (b) for a point inside (r < R). Both surfaces are concentric spheres.

subsections.title: Field Outside the Shell (r > R)
subsections.content_text: For a point $P$ outside the shell, we use a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$. The electric field at each point is parallel to the radius vector and has constant magnitude $E$. The flux is $E \times 4\pi r^2$. The charge enclosed is $q = \sigma \times 4\pi R^2$. Gauss’s law gives $E \times 4\pi r^2 = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0} 4\pi R^2$, leading to $E = \frac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2}$. Vectorially, $\mathbf{E} = \frac{q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}$ (1.34). The field is as if the entire charge is concentrated at the center.

subsections.title: Field Inside the Shell (r < R)
subsections.content_text: For a point $P$ inside the shell, the Gaussian surface is a sphere of radius $r < R$. The flux is $E \times 4\pi r^2$, but the enclosed charge is zero. Gauss’s law gives $E \times 4\pi r^2 = 0$, so $E = 0$ (1.35). The field is zero at all points inside the shell. This result confirms the $1/r^2$ dependence in Coulomb’s law and is analogous to the gravitational field inside a uniform mass shell (compare with Section 7.5 of Class XI Textbook of Physics).

subsections.title: Example 1.12: Atomic Model Field Calculation
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the electric field for an early atomic model with a point nucleus and a uniform negative charge cloud. It involves determining the charge density and applying Gauss's law to spherical surfaces for points inside and outside the charge distribution.

worked_examples.label: Example 1.12
worked_examples.problem: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge $Ze$, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge up to a radius $R$. The atom as a whole is neutral. For this model, what is the electric field at a distance $r$ from the nucleus?
worked_examples.solution: The total negative charge must be $-Ze$ for neutrality. The negative charge density $\rho$ is: $\rho = -\frac{3Ze}{4\pi R^3}$. Using a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$, the flux is $\phi = E(r) \times 4\pi r^2$. (i) For $r < R$: The electric flux $\phi$ enclosed by the spherical surface is $\phi = E(r) \times 4\pi r^2$ [CONTINUES]

diagrams.label: Figure 1.29
diagrams.description: Model of an atom with a positive nucleus and a uniform spherical cloud of negative charge of radius R. Point P is at distance r.

subsections.title: Example 1.12: Field Calculation (Continued)
subsections.content_text:
The derivation for the electric field $E(r)$ of the atomic model continues by determining the charge $q$ enclosed by a Gaussian surface of radius $r$. The field at any point on the spherical Gaussian surface has the same direction as the normal to the surface and the same magnitude at all points. 

For the case (i) $r < R$, the charge $q$ enclosed is the sum of the positive nuclear charge and the negative charge within the sphere of radius $r$: 
$$q = Ze + \frac{4\pi r^3}{3} \rho$$
Substituting the charge density $\rho$ obtained earlier, we have:
$$q = Ze - Ze \frac{r^3}{R^3}$$
Applying Gauss's law, the electric field is:
$$E(r) = \frac{Ze}{4\pi \epsilon_0} \left( \frac{1}{r^2} - \frac{r}{R^3} \right) ; r < R$$
The electric field is directed radially outward. 

For the case (ii) $r > R$, the total charge enclosed by the Gaussian spherical surface is zero since the atom is neutral. Thus, from Gauss's law:
$$E(r) \times 4\pi r^2 = 0 \implies E(r) = 0 ; r > R$$
At the boundary $r = R$, both cases yield the same result: $E = 0$.

sections.title: Summary of Electric Charges and Fields

subsections.title: Fundamental Nature of Electric Charge and Forces
subsections.content_text: Electric and magnetic forces are fundamental interactions that determine the properties of atoms, molecules, and bulk matter. Experimental evidence from frictional electricity shows there are two types of charges in nature. By convention, the charge on a glass rod rubbed with silk is positive, while the charge on a plastic rod rubbed with fur is negative. Like charges repel each other, and unlike charges attract.

subsections.title: Conductors, Insulators, and Basic Properties of Charge
subsections.content_text:
Materials are classified as conductors if they allow the movement of electric charge (e.g., metals via mobile electrons, electrolytes via mobile ions) and insulators if they do not. Electric charge has three basic properties:
1. **Quantisation**: The total charge $q$ of a body is always an integral multiple of a basic quantum of charge $e$ ($q = ne$, where $n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$). For macroscopic charges where $n$ is very large, quantisation can be ignored.
2. **Additivity**: The total charge of a system is the algebraic sum of all individual charges, taking into account their proper signs.
3. **Conservation**: The total charge of an isolated system remains unchanged with time. Charging involves the transfer of charge between bodies, not its creation or destruction.

subsections.title: Coulomb's Law and Superposition Principle
subsections.content_text:
Coulomb's Law states that the mutual electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ is proportional to the product $q_1 q_2$ and inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r_{21}$ separating them:
$$\mathbf{F}_{21} = \frac{k (q_1 q_2)}{r_{21}^2} \mathbf{\hat{r}}_{21}$$
where $k = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \approx 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2 \text{ C}^{-2}$ and $\epsilon_0 \approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2 \text{ N}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-2}$. The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between a proton and an electron is approximately $2.4 \times 10^{39}$. The Superposition Principle states that the force on any charge is the vector sum of the forces exerted by each individual charge in a system.

subsections.title: Electric Field and Field Lines
subsections.content_text: The electric field $\mathbf{E}$ at a point is the force on a small positive test charge $q$ divided by the magnitude of that charge. For a point charge, the field magnitude is $E = |q|/4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2$, directed radially outward for positive charges and inward for negative ones. Electric field lines are visual tools where the tangent at any point gives the field direction. They are continuous curves that do not cross, start at positive charges, end at negative charges, and do not form closed loops. Their relative closeness indicates the field's strength.

subsections.title: Electric Dipoles and Torque
subsections.content_text:
An electric dipole is a pair of equal and opposite charges $q$ and $-q$ separated by distance $2a$. The dipole moment vector $\mathbf{p}$ has magnitude $2qa$ and points from $-q$ to $+q$. The field of a dipole at large distances ($r \gg a$) falls off as $1/r^3$:
- **Equatorial plane**: $\mathbf{E} = \frac{-\mathbf{p}}{4\pi\epsilon_0 (a^2 + r^2)^{3/2}} \cong \frac{-\mathbf{p}}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^3}$
- **Axial line**: $\mathbf{E} = \frac{2\mathbf{p}r}{4\pi\epsilon_0 (r^2 - a^2)^2} \cong \frac{2\mathbf{p}}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^3}$
In a uniform external field $\mathbf{E}$, a dipole experiences a torque $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{p} \times \mathbf{E}$ but no net force.

subsections.title: Electric Flux and Gauss's Law
subsections.content_text:
Electric flux $\Delta \phi$ through an area element $\Delta \mathbf{S}$ is $\Delta \phi = \mathbf{E} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{S}$, where $\Delta \mathbf{S} = \Delta S \mathbf{\hat{n}}$. Gauss's Law states that the total flux through any closed surface $S$ is $1/\epsilon_0$ times the total charge enclosed. It is particularly useful for symmetric charge distributions:
- **Infinitely long straight wire**: $\mathbf{E} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\epsilon_0 r} \mathbf{\hat{n}}$ (where $\lambda$ is linear charge density and $\mathbf{\hat{n}}$ is the radial unit vector).
- **Infinite thin plane sheet**: $\mathbf{E} = \frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0} \mathbf{\hat{n}}$ (where $\sigma$ is surface charge density and $\mathbf{\hat{n}}$ is the unit vector normal to the plane).

subsections.title: Field due to a Thin Spherical Shell
subsections.content_text: The electric field $\mathbf{E}$ due to a thin spherical shell of uniform surface charge density $\sigma$ and radius $R$ is characterized by two regions: (i) Outside the shell ($r \ge R$), the field is $\mathbf{E} = \frac{q}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}$, where $q = 4\pi R^2 \sigma$ is the total charge. This indicates that the field outside the shell is as though the total charge is concentrated at the centre. This same result applies to a solid sphere of uniform volume charge density. (ii) Inside the shell ($r < R$), the electric field is zero ($\mathbf{E} = 0$) at all points.

subsections.title: Nuclear Forces and Atomic Structure
subsections.content_text: Protons, despite carrying positive charges, reside compactly inside the nucleus due to the strong nuclear force, which is a third kind of fundamental force. This force is effective only over very small distances (~$10^{-14}$ m), which is the size of the nucleus. Electrons do not reside inside the nucleus because the laws of quantum mechanics prevent them from sitting on top of protons, giving atoms their structure.

subsections.title: Comparison of Electrostatic and Gravitational Forces
subsections.content_text: Both Coulomb and gravitational forces follow the inverse-square law ($1/r^2$). However, gravitational force is always attractive, while Coulomb force can be both attractive and repulsive. Despite being much weaker, gravity can be a dominating force in nature because electrostatic forces often cancel out due to the presence of both positive and negative charges.

subsections.title: The Proportionality Constant and SI Units
subsections.content_text: The constant $k$ in Coulomb's law is determined by the choice of the unit of charge. In SI units, the unit of current (Ampere) is defined via its magnetic effect, and the unit of charge (Coulomb) is defined as $1 \text{ C} = 1 \text{ A s}$. Consequently, $k$ is not arbitrary and is approximately $9 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2 \text{ C}^{-2}$. A charge of $1 \text{ C}$ is quite large because magnetic forces are generally much weaker than electric forces.

subsections.title: Properties of Charge: Scalar Nature and Invariance
subsections.content_text: Electric charge is a scalar quantity and possesses an additive property. It is invariant under rotation and remains constant across different frames of reference in relative motion (unlike quantities like kinetic energy). The total charge of an isolated system is conserved over time, which is a property independent of its scalar nature.

subsections.title: Quantisation and Superposition
subsections.content_text: Quantisation of electric charge is a basic law of nature with no known analogue for mass. The superposition principle states that the force on one charge due to another is unaffected by the presence of additional charges. This principle allows for the calculation of total force in multi-body systems by vector addition.

subsections.title: Electric Field Definitions and Neutral Configurations
subsections.content_text: The electric field due to discrete charges is not defined at the charge locations themselves. For continuous distributions, it is defined everywhere, though it is discontinuous across a surface charge distribution. Even if a configuration has a total charge of zero, its electric field is not necessarily zero; however, at large distances, the field of such neutral configurations (like dipoles) falls off faster than the $1/r^2$ rate typical of a single point charge.

subsections.title: Visual Representation of Exercise Scenarios
subsections.content_text: The following figures provide visual context for the exercises in this chapter. Figure 1.30 shows the paths of three charged particles in a uniform electric field established between two oppositely charged plates. The top plate is positively charged, and the bottom plate is negatively charged. Particles 1 and 2 are deflected upwards toward the positive plate, while particle 3 is deflected downwards toward the negative plate. Figure 1.31 illustrates a point charge positioned above a square surface, a configuration used to calculate electric flux through symmetry by considering the square as one face of a cube.

diagrams.description: A diagram showing three numbered paths (1, 2, 3) of particles moving horizontally into a region between two parallel charged plates. The top plate is marked with '+' signs and the bottom plate with '-' signs. Paths 1 and 2 curve upwards towards the positive plate, while path 3 curves downwards towards the negative plate. A coordinate system with x and y axes is shown.

items.problem: What is the force between two small charged spheres having charges of $2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$ and $3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$ placed $30 \text{ cm}$ apart in air?

The electrostatic force on a small sphere of charge $0.4 \mu\text{C}$ due to another small sphere of charge $-0.8 \mu\text{C}$ in air is $0.2 \text{ N}$. (a) What is the distance between the two spheres? (b) What is the force on the second sphere due to the first?
Check that the ratio $ke^2/G m_e m_p$ is dimensionless. Look up a Table of Physical Constants and determine the value of this ratio. What does the ratio signify?
(a) Explain the meaning of the statement 'electric charge of a body is quantised'. (b) Why can one ignore quantisation of electric charge when dealing with macroscopic i.e., large scale charges?

When a glass rod is rubbed with a silk cloth, charges appear on both. A similar phenomenon is observed with many other pairs of bodies. Explain how this observation is consistent with the law of conservation of charge.

Four point charges $q_A = 2 \mu\text{C}$, $q_B = -5 \mu\text{C}$, $q_C = 2 \mu\text{C}$, and $q_D = -5 \mu\text{C}$ are located at the corners of a square $ABCD$ of side $10 \text{ cm}$. What is the force on a charge of $1 \mu\text{C}$ placed at the centre of the square?
(a) An electrostatic field line is a continuous curve. That is, a field line cannot have sudden breaks. Why not? (b) Explain why two field lines never cross each other at any point?

Two point charges $q_A = 3 \mu\text{C}$ and $q_B = -3 \mu\text{C}$ are located $20 \text{ cm}$ apart in vacuum. (a) What is the electric field at the midpoint $O$ of the line $AB$ joining the two charges? (b) If a negative test charge of magnitude $1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C}$ is placed at this point, what is the force experienced by the test charge?

A system has two charges $q_A = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$ and $q_B = -2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$ located at points $A: (0, 0, -15 \text{ cm})$ and $B: (0, 0, +15 \text{ cm})$, respectively. What are the total charge and electric dipole moment of the system?

An electric dipole with dipole moment $4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C m}$ is aligned at $30^\circ$ with the direction of a uniform electric field of magnitude $5 \times 10^4 \text{ N C}^{-1}$. Calculate the magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole.

A polythene piece rubbed with wool is found to have a negative charge of $3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$. (a) Estimate the number of electrons transferred (from which to which?). (b) Is there a transfer of mass from wool to polythene?

(a) Two insulated charged copper spheres $A$ and $B$ have their centres separated by a distance of $50 \text{ cm}$. What is the mutual force of electrostatic repulsion if the charge on each is $6.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C}$? The radii of $A$ and $B$ are negligible compared to the distance of separation. (b) What is the force of repulsion if each sphere is charged double the above amount, and the distance between them is halved?
Figure 1.30 shows tracks of three charged particles in a uniform electrostatic field. Give the signs of the three charges. Which particle has the highest charge to mass ratio?

Consider a uniform electric field $\mathbf{E} = 3 \times 10^3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}$ N/C. (a) What is the flux of this field through a square of 10 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the $yz$ plane? (b) What is the flux through the same square if the normal to its plane makes a $60^\circ$ angle with the $x$-axis?
What is the net flux of the uniform electric field of Exercise 1.14 through a cube of side 20 cm oriented so that its faces are parallel to the coordinate planes?

Careful measurement of the electric field at the surface of a black box indicates that the net outward flux through the surface of the box is $8.0 \times 10^3$ Nm$^2$/C. (a) What is the net charge inside the box? (b) If the net outward flux through the surface of the box were zero, could you conclude that there were no charges inside the box? Why or Why not?

A point charge $+10 \mu$C is a distance 5 cm directly above the centre of a square of side 10 cm, as shown in Fig. 1.31. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square? (Hint: Think of the square as one face of a cube with edge 10 cm.)
A point charge of $2.0 \mu$C is at the centre of a cubic Gaussian surface 9.0 cm on edge. What is the net electric flux through the surface?

A point charge causes an electric flux of $-1.0 \times 10^3$ Nm$^2$/C to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 10.0 cm radius centred on the charge. (a) If the radius of the Gaussian surface were doubled, how much flux would pass through the surface? (b) What is the value of the point charge?

A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm has an unknown charge. If the electric field 20 cm from the centre of the sphere is $1.5 \times 10^3$ N/C and points radially inward, what is the net charge on the sphere?

A uniformly charged conducting sphere of 2.4 m diameter has a surface charge density of $80.0 \mu$C/m$^2$. (a) Find the charge on the sphere. (b) What is the total electric flux leaving the surface of the sphere?
An infinite line charge produces a field of $9 \times 10^4$ N/C at a distance of 2 cm. Calculate the linear charge density.

Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude $17.0 \times 10^{-22}$ C/m$^2$. What is $\mathbf{E}$: (a) in the outer region of the first plate, (b) in the outer region of the second plate, and (c) between the plates?
